s1_b1: A boulder you cannot possibly lift. Slide a bar under it, rest it on a rock, and push the far end down.
s1_b2: The boulder rises. With a force far smaller than its weight. It feels like cheating.
s1_b3: It isn't. Watch your hand, not the rock. Your end traveled a long way down.
s1_b4: The boulder barely moved. You pushed gently — but through a huge distance.
s1_b5: That's the bargain every machine makes. It trades force for distance. It never gives you both.
s1_b6: The factor by which it multiplies your force has a name. The mechanical advantage.
s1_b7: A ramp is the same deal. Sliding a barrel up a long slope is easy — but you walk much farther.
s1_b8: So is a screw, an axle, a pulley. All of them: less force, more distance, same job underneath.
s1_b9: Stack them together and you get a compound machine — a bicycle, a pair of scissors, an engine.
s1_b10: In a perfect machine, what you put in equals what you get out. Force times distance, conserved.
s1_b11: The machine never makes energy. It only reshapes the deal — so a human can do a job a human couldn't.
s1_b12: Computing the mechanical advantage of a lever or ramp from its dimensions, and the input force a given load needs — that's yours.
